sentence: Subjective:
The patient, Betty, is a 30-year-old female who presents with chronic asthma symptoms, including coughing and wheezing, which she describes as persistent and worsening over the past six months.
She reports that her symptoms are triggered by getting sick, particularly with colds, and that she has had several exacerbations in the past year, including one hospitalization as a child.

She is currently using an albuterol inhaler, Ventolin, two puffs as needed, and reports that it provides relief, but she is concerned about her frequent use of the inhaler and the impact on her daily life.
She has also noted that she has trouble with her asthma when she visits her aunt's house due to her cat.
Past medical history: The patient has a history of asthma, with multiple exacerbations in the past year, including one hospitalization as a child.
She has been using an albuterol inhaler, Ventolin, for her asthma symptoms.
Review of systems: The patient reports no other symptoms or concerns outside of her asthma.
Current medications:
Albuterol inhaler (Ventolin), 2 puffs as needed
Flovent (fluticasone propionate), 110 micrograms, 1 puff, twice daily, starting today
Objective:
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.
Physical exam: The patient's lung exam reveals diminished lung sounds with occasional slight expiratory wheeze, bilaterally.
The trachea is midline, and the neck is supple.
Test Results:
Pulmonary function test results are consistent with asthma.
Assessment / Problem List:
Assessment: The patient's symptoms are consistent with moderate intermittent asthma.
Problem list: Asthma; Asthma, moderate intermittent, active; Asthma, suspected, past (hospitalization as a child); Asthma, active
Plan:
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms, 1 puff, twice daily, to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce the need for rescue inhaler use.
I instructed the patient to continue using her Ventolin inhaler as needed, 2 puffs every 4-6 hours.
I also created an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
I will see the patient in 30 days to review her progress.
Follow-up: The patient will be seen in 30 days to review her asthma action plan and assess her symptoms.
